title: Practice Exam #4 (Cloud Database Engineer)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Cloud Database Engineer - 50 questions

questions.text: A healthcare analytics platform processes millions of patient record queries daily through a Google Cloud Spanner instance configured as a regional deployment with three nodes. The organization is preparing to onboard five new hospital systems simultaneously next month, which will triple the normal query and transaction volume over a sustained two-week period. The engineering team needs to ensure that both read and write performance remain within acceptable latency thresholds during this onboarding window without modifying the existing database schema or application logic. What should the team do?

options.text: Add more nodes to the existing Spanner instance
options.explanation:
Adding nodes to the current Spanner instance is the most direct and effective way to handle a predictable surge in transaction volume. When you scale up the node count, Spanner automatically rebalances data splits across the new compute resources, increasing the available CPU and memory to handle higher throughput. This rebalancing happens transparently with no downtime, meaning the platform continues serving requests normally throughout the change. Because the question specifies no changes to schema or application code, this approach is ideal since it only requires an infrastructure adjustment. Once the onboarding period concludes and traffic normalizes, the team can scale the nodes back down.
Migrating to a multi-region configuration changes the entire availability and replication model of the instance and typically introduces higher write latency because data must be replicated across geographically distant locations. This is an architectural shift better suited to global availability requirements, not a temporary throughput increase. Setting up read-only replicas in another region would help distribute read traffic geographically but does nothing to increase write capacity on the primary instance, which is critical when transaction volume triples. Restructuring the database with interleaved tables is a schema design optimization that co-locates parent and child rows, but it requires schema modifications and potentially significant data migration effort, which directly contradicts the constraint of not changing the existing schema.

questions.text: A healthcare technology startup is building a mobile application that allows field nurses to record patient vitals and observations during home visits in rural areas with unreliable cellular coverage. The collected data must be available locally on the device during visits and then automatically sync to the cloud when connectivity returns. The company wants to keep development and infrastructure management effort to a minimum by leveraging Google Cloud managed services. What database architecture should you recommend?

options.text: Use Firebase with Cloud Firestore as the database layer within the mobile application on each device
options.explanation:
Cloud Firestore through Firebase is the ideal solution because its client SDKs provide built-in offline persistence that automatically caches data on the device and synchronizes changes to the cloud once network connectivity is restored. This means field nurses can continue recording patient information during home visits in areas with poor cellular reception, and the application will seamlessly push that data to the cloud backend without any custom synchronization code. Firebase as a platform also reduces operational overhead significantly because there are no database instances to provision or manage on individual devices or in the cloud, and it scales naturally as the number of field workers grows.
The option involving SQLite with Cloud SQL requires you to build and maintain your own offline caching and data synchronization logic, which adds substantial development complexity and ongoing operational burden that the company wants to avoid. Similarly, using Room or Core Data with AlloyDB means you are responsible for designing a reliable sync mechanism between the local database and the cloud, and AlloyDB itself does not offer any client-side offline capabilities. The option suggesting Cloud Spanner as the backend still leaves the offline data problem unsolved because Cloud Spanner has no client-side SDK with offline support, and its globally distributed architecture is far more than what this use case demands. Each of these alternatives essentially requires the team to recreate functionality that Firestore already provides natively.

questions.text: Your team recently migrated an on-premises PostgreSQL database to Cloud SQL. One of the legacy processes was a nightly script that exported a specific set of tables to a storage location, triggered by a cron job on the database server. Now that the database server no longer exists, you need to replicate this scheduled export in Google Cloud. You want to minimize both the amount of infrastructure you manage and the ongoing costs. What should you do?

options.text: Create a Dataflow pipeline that continuously monitors and exports the tables
options.explanation:
The best solution here is to combine Cloud Scheduler with Cloud Functions, both of which are fully managed serverless services. Cloud Scheduler replaces the traditional cron job by firing an event at your chosen time each night. The Cloud Function receives that trigger and runs the export logic, then shuts down immediately after completion. Because neither service requires you to provision or maintain servers, the operational burden is extremely low. You also only pay when the function actually runs, which in this case is just once per night, making it the most cost-effective option.
A Compute Engine instance running a cron job would work functionally but means you are paying around the clock for a virtual machine that only does meaningful work once per day. You would also be responsible for patching the operating system and keeping the instance healthy. Cloud Composer is a managed Apache Airflow environment that is well suited to complex multi-step orchestration, but it carries a significant baseline monthly cost and introduces operational complexity that is unnecessary when the task is a single nightly export. A continuously running Dataflow pipeline is designed to handle large-scale streaming or batch data processing and would be both excessive in capability and expensive relative to the simplicity of this requirement.

questions.text: Your company operates a financial reporting platform backed by a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance configured with High Availability. Earlier today, Google published an urgent maintenance update that resolves a critical authentication vulnerability. The automatic maintenance is currently scheduled for next Wednesday, but your security team has mandated that the fix must be deployed tonight between 11 PM and 1 AM to meet regulatory obligations. What is the most efficient approach to ensure the primary and all associated standby instances receive the patch before business hours tomorrow?

options.text: Manually run the self-service maintenance on each standby and failover replica individually
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use the self-service maintenance capability directly on the primary Cloud SQL instance during the approved maintenance window. When an administrator initiates self-service maintenance on the primary instance, Cloud SQL automatically orchestrates the update across the entire instance cluster, including high availability standbys and any read replicas. This means the administrator does not need to individually manage each node, and the platform ensures version consistency and data integrity throughout the process. By performing this action within the 11 PM to 1 AM window, the team satisfies the security mandate while relying on the managed service to handle the coordination of updates across all associated instances.
Changing the recurring maintenance window only adjusts when future scheduled maintenance will occur and does not cause a currently pending update to be applied immediately. Switching the maintenance track to the preview channel controls which feature releases an instance receives over time and is not a mechanism to force a specific pending security patch onto the instance on demand. Attempting to manually trigger self-service maintenance on each standby or failover replica separately is not the intended workflow in Cloud SQL, as maintenance sequencing is designed to originate from the primary instance, and trying to update replicas independently risks version mismatches and added operational complexity.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company is building a clinical research platform on Google Cloud that uses Cloud Spanner as its primary database. Researchers need the ability to search through millions of medical journal abstracts and clinical trial summaries to discover studies that are conceptually related to their current research topics, even when different terminology is used across disciplines. The platform must deliver fast query responses and avoid introducing additional infrastructure or data synchronization overhead. What is the best approach to meet these requirements?

options.text: Enable a Full Text Search index on the abstract and summary columns and use the SEARCH function with a natural language query
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use Spanner's native vector search capability, which allows the company to store machine learning embeddings alongside their relational data and query them directly within the same database. When researchers need to find conceptually related studies, the system can compare vector representations of the text rather than relying on exact keyword overlap. The VECTOR_SEARCH function with the Euclidean distance metric calculates how close two points are in a high-dimensional embedding space, which captures semantic meaning that goes beyond surface-level word matching. This means that even when different medical disciplines use entirely different vocabulary to describe similar concepts, the system can still identify relevant connections. Keeping everything within Spanner eliminates the need to manage a separate search infrastructure and avoids data movement latency.
Full Text Search indexes and the SEARCH function are built around keyword matching, stemming, and linguistic analysis, which means they struggle when researchers use different terminology than what appears in the original abstracts. A standard SQL secondary index with LIKE queries and wildcard matching can only find exact character patterns and has no understanding of the meaning behind words, making it unsuitable for semantic discovery across varied scientific language. Exporting the data to a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance to use the pgvector extension would introduce unnecessary architectural complexity, require ongoing data synchronization between two databases, and add latency that conflicts with the requirement to avoid additional infrastructure overhead. Each of these alternatives either fails to address the core need of semantic similarity search or adds operational burden that could be avoided by using the vector capabilities already built into Spanner.

questions.text: A global insurance company runs a transaction-processing system on a multi-region Cloud Spanner deployment. The application handles a very high volume of insert and update operations with almost no read queries. The database engineering team has observed that transaction commit times are slower than expected and wants to reduce this latency. What should the team do?

options.text: Build indexes on the most frequently written columns
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to turn off the leader-aware routing feature in the Cloud Spanner client library. In a multi-region configuration, leader-aware routing causes the client to track which replica currently holds the leader role and directs traffic specifically to that node. While this can benefit certain read patterns, it introduces additional overhead when the workload is dominated by writes. The client spends time maintaining metadata about leader transitions, and this extra processing adds latency to every transaction commit. By disabling this feature, the client sends requests to the nearest available replica and lets the Spanner backend handle internal routing to the leader. Google recommends this optimization specifically when workloads are write-heavy and do not benefit from client-side leader tracking.
Enabling leader-aware routing would make the situation worse because it increases the client-side processing burden without providing any benefit to a write-dominated workload. Adding read-only replicas across more regions is a strategy that improves read scalability and availability, but it has no effect on the speed of write commits since writes must still go through the leader replica regardless of how many read replicas exist. Creating indexes on frequently written columns would actually degrade write performance further because each index must be updated and synchronized across regions every time a row is inserted or modified, adding more work to each transaction rather than reducing it.

questions.text: A SaaS company operates a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance that powers its customer-facing analytics dashboard. After onboarding several enterprise clients, the operations team noticed that the dashboard response times have degraded significantly during business hours. Investigation reveals that the high volume of concurrent SELECT queries from dashboard users is overwhelming the database, causing delays in order processing and inventory updates handled by the same instance. The team needs a solution that improves read performance without disrupting the write workload on the primary database. What should the team implement?

options.text: Configure a Cloud SQL read replica and modify the application to route SELECT queries to the replica endpoint
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to create a read replica and redirect the dashboard read traffic to that replica. Cloud SQL read replicas allow you to offload read-only queries from the primary instance, freeing it to handle transactional write operations like order processing and inventory updates. When the application is configured to send SELECT queries to the replica endpoint, the read workload is distributed across separate compute resources. This is the standard architectural pattern in relational databases when read-heavy traffic begins to compete with write operations on a single instance.
The Auth Proxy is a connectivity and authentication tool that simplifies how applications connect to Cloud SQL, but it does not add compute capacity or distribute query load across multiple database instances. Enabling high availability creates a standby instance that only activates during a failover event, meaning it does not serve any traffic under normal conditions and would not reduce the read pressure on the primary. Upgrading to a larger machine type may temporarily alleviate the problem, but vertical scaling has a hard upper limit, typically requires a restart that causes brief downtime, and does not address the fundamental issue of read and write workloads competing on a single instance.

questions.text: A large online gaming company relies on Memorystore for Redis to cache player state and matchmaking data across active game sessions. The engineering team has been tasked with designing a disaster recovery strategy that addresses the possibility of a complete regional outage. In the event of such a failure, traffic will be redirected to a secondary region where a new Memorystore instance must be populated with the latest available cache data from the primary site. What approach should the team implement to ensure the standby region can restore the cache with the most recent data?

options.text: Export the Redis data to a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to export the Redis data to a Cloud Storage bucket configured with dual-region or multi-region availability. This ensures the exported RDB file persists and remains accessible even during a full regional failure. When traffic shifts to the secondary region, the operations team can import this file into a newly provisioned Memorystore instance, restoring the cache to a recent state rather than starting cold with an empty cache. This export and import workflow can be automated on a schedule to keep the backup data as fresh as possible.
Provisioning a Standard Tier instance with read replicas provides redundancy across zones within a single region, but it does not replicate data to a geographically separate region, so a total regional outage would make all replicas unavailable. Enabling High Availability on a Redis cluster similarly protects against zonal failures by maintaining replicas in different zones within the same region, but it offers no protection against a scenario where the entire region becomes unreachable. Configuring RDB snapshots at a high frequency captures point-in-time backups of the data, but those snapshots are managed by the service backend within the same region and cannot be natively accessed or imported into an instance running in a different region. None of these three alternatives address the fundamental requirement of making cache data available outside the primary region during a regional disaster.

questions.text: A mid-sized financial services company is shutting down its co-located data center and must transfer multiple PostgreSQL and MySQL databases to Cloud SQL. The operations team has limited bandwidth for creating full database exports locally and needs to ensure near-zero downtime during the transition. The destination databases must stay current with production changes until the final switchover. Which two approaches should you use to accomplish this migration?

options.text: Configure replication from an external source server
options.explanation:
Database Migration Service and replication from an external source server together provide the right combination of tooling and mechanism to meet these requirements. Database Migration Service offers a fully managed workflow that orchestrates the initial data load and then maintains continuous replication between the on-premises source and the Cloud SQL target. This avoids the need to generate large local dump files because data streams directly from the source to the destination. Replication from an external source server is the technical foundation that allows the Cloud SQL instance to function as a replica of the on-premises database, continuously applying changes until you are ready to promote it and complete the cutover. Both approaches work together to minimize downtime and eliminate the dependency on local storage capacity.
A cross-region read replica is designed to replicate data between Google Cloud regions and assumes the primary instance is already running inside Cloud SQL, so it does not apply to an on-premises source. A standard read replica similarly operates within the Cloud SQL environment and is intended to distribute read traffic across additional instances rather than to ingest data from an external data center. While an external read replica might seem relevant due to its name, that feature is intended to create a replica outside of Cloud SQL from an existing Cloud SQL primary, which is the opposite direction of what this scenario requires. None of these three alternatives provide the connectivity model or replication configuration needed to pull data from an on-premises database into Cloud SQL while keeping it synchronized through the migration window.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup maintains a Cloud SQL for MySQL database containing millions of patient feedback entries collected from clinics nationwide. They are building an intelligent search feature that enables medical researchers to locate feedback entries by describing emotional tone or patient sentiment in plain language, without needing to know the exact phrases patients used. What is the best approach to implement this search functionality?

options.text: Implement a database trigger that applies JSON_EXTRACT to parse and compare sentiment tags stored in metadata fields
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to convert the feedback text into vector embeddings and then perform a nearest neighbor search against the researcher's query. Cloud SQL for MySQL supports storing high-dimensional vectors, and by transforming both the stored feedback and the researcher's natural language description into numerical representations, the system can calculate mathematical similarity between them. This means a researcher can describe a sentiment like "patients feeling dismissed by staff" and receive relevant results even if none of the feedback entries contain those exact words. Nearest neighbor search finds the vectors most similar to the input, effectively bridging vocabulary differences and capturing semantic meaning.
A full-text search index with MATCH AGAINST works by matching keywords and linguistic stems, which means it would struggle to connect a researcher's description of emotional tone with patient feedback that uses entirely different vocabulary to express the same sentiment. Computing Levenshtein distance measures how many single-character edits are needed to transform one string into another, making it useful only for catching typos or minor spelling variations rather than understanding meaning. Using JSON_EXTRACT with a trigger assumes that sentiment categories have already been identified and stored as structured tags in a metadata column, which does not help when researchers want to search using freeform, open-ended descriptions. None of these alternatives can perform the kind of semantic matching that vector embeddings naturally enable.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm maintains a Cloud Firestore database that tracks patient appointment records across multiple clinics. The compliance team has mandated that a complete database export must be performed every night at 2:00 AM and stored in a Cloud Storage bucket to satisfy audit requirements. The engineering team wants the most hands-off and cost-efficient solution possible, avoiding any persistent infrastructure. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function that initiates the Firestore export
options.explanation:
The best approach here is pairing Cloud Scheduler with a Cloud Function to automate the nightly Firestore export. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron service that can fire at exactly 2:00 AM each night, and the Cloud Function provides lightweight serverless compute to call the Firestore export API. Because the function only runs for a few seconds per invocation, the cost is negligible compared to keeping any infrastructure running around the clock. This combination requires no server provisioning, no OS patching, and no container image management. Built-in retry logic in Cloud Scheduler also means the team does not need to write custom error-handling workflows to deal with transient failures.
Provisioning a Compute Engine instance means paying for a virtual machine that sits idle for nearly the entire day just to run a brief script once at night, and the team would need to maintain the operating system and monitor the instance health. An App Engine cron job can technically schedule tasks, but it introduces more setup complexity than necessary when the only requirement is a single lightweight API call each night. A Cloud Run container would work functionally, but it adds the overhead of building and maintaining a container image, which is unnecessary packaging effort when a simple function accomplishes the same goal. Any solution that depends on persistent infrastructure also introduces the risk of missed backups if that infrastructure goes offline unexpectedly.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that supports a high-volume transaction processing system. During business hours, the application experiences sporadic slowdowns that seem to correlate with periods of heavy write activity. The database engineering team suspects that transactions may be waiting on locks held by other sessions, and they also want to confirm whether the underlying instance is running out of compute capacity during these spikes. What combination of tools and configurations should you use to identify the source of these performance degradations?

options.text: Query the pg_stat_activity view, examine the auto-explain extension logs, and monitor instance storage capacity in the Google Cloud Console
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use System Insights alongside the log_lock_waits database flag and Cloud Monitoring resource metrics. System Insights provides a dedicated dashboard that surfaces database-level performance data and helps pinpoint specific bottlenecks such as lock contention. Enabling the log_lock_waits flag causes PostgreSQL to log whenever a session waits longer than the deadlock timeout to acquire a lock, which is exactly what you need when investigating whether concurrent transactions are blocking each other during heavy write periods. Cloud Monitoring lets you correlate those lock wait events with CPU utilization trends and active connection counts, helping you determine whether the instance is simply under-provisioned or whether the slowdowns stem from inefficient transactional logic.
Using log_min_duration_statement and Cloud Trace is oriented toward identifying individually slow queries and tracing latency through the application stack, but neither of these directly reveals lock contention between concurrent database sessions. Querying pg_stat_activity can show currently running sessions in real time, but it does not provide the persistent historical logging of lock waits needed to catch intermittent issues that happen unpredictably throughout the day, and monitoring storage capacity is unrelated to the write contention problem described. Configuring Query Insights and enabling log_connections helps you understand query patterns and when clients connect, but neither captures wait event data, which is the critical signal when diagnosing transactional blocking, and searching Cloud Logging specifically for memory exhaustion events targets a different category of problem entirely.

questions.text: A SaaS company hosts its multi-tenant backend on Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. The engineering team has noticed that database costs have been climbing steadily, but they are unsure which tenant workloads or application features are responsible. Leadership wants a repeatable approach that pinpoints the most resource-intensive database activity so the team can right-size the instance tier and avoid paying for unused capacity. What should the team do to achieve ongoing cost optimization of their Cloud SQL environment?

options.text: Use the Recommender API to automatically enforce suggested modifications to database configuration flags
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage the native Query Insights feature in Cloud SQL, which gives the team direct visibility into which specific SQL statements are consuming the most CPU, I/O, and time. Rather than guessing which tenants or features are driving up costs, Query Insights surfaces the actual database operations responsible. This allows the engineering team to make data-driven decisions about whether to optimize application queries, restructure indexes, or adjust the instance machine type. Because the tool is built into Cloud SQL, it requires no custom infrastructure and can be used on an ongoing basis to continuously align instance capacity with real workload demand.
Building custom Python scripts to parse logs and trigger automated scaling introduces unnecessary complexity and does not address the underlying cause of high costs, which may be inefficient queries rather than insufficient resources. Cloud Monitoring alerts tied to memory utilization only show infrastructure-level metrics and cannot reveal which SQL statements are responsible, so any scaling decisions based on those alerts tend to be imprecise and lead to over-provisioning. Manual machine type changes in response to alerts are reactive and teams frequently forget to scale back down after peak periods pass, which defeats the goal of cost optimization. The Recommender API can offer helpful suggestions, but it lacks the ability to autonomously apply changes to instance configurations without additional automation built around it, and it still does not provide the query-level detail needed to understand what is actually driving resource consumption.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company operates multiple production databases on Google Cloud. Their appointment scheduling system uses Cloud SQL, and their billing platform stores transaction records in Cloud Spanner. The analytics team has been receiving frequent requests from department managers who need to build dashboards and run ad hoc queries against specific fields from both systems. The company does not want to maintain data pipelines that move data between systems, and security policy prohibits sharing database credentials with non-engineering staff. The company has standardized on BigQuery as the primary interface where business users run queries. The team needs a solution that allows managers to access only designated tables and columns from the operational databases through BigQuery without duplicating the underlying data. What should the team do?

options.text: Build BigQuery views that leverage EXTERNAL_QUERY to read from the operational databases and grant managers access only to those views
options.explanation:
BigQuery's federated query capability through the EXTERNAL_QUERY function enables queries to run against external databases like Cloud SQL and Cloud Spanner without ingesting the data into BigQuery storage. The team can author BigQuery views containing EXTERNAL_QUERY statements that reference only the approved tables and columns in the operational systems. Managers then receive access to these views rather than the databases themselves. This satisfies the requirement to centralize access through BigQuery while keeping data in place and avoiding credential sharing. The views serve as a governance boundary that controls exactly which fields are visible to business users.
Setting up scheduled export jobs would create copies of the data in BigQuery, which directly contradicts the requirement to avoid maintaining data pipelines and duplicating data across systems. Scheduled pipelines also introduce refresh lag and operational overhead that the team explicitly wants to avoid. Creating read replicas in separate environments still requires provisioning infrastructure and distributing database credentials to non-engineering users, violating the security policy. It also fails to route access through BigQuery as the standardized query interface. Configuring dashboard tools to connect directly to the operational databases exposes production systems to additional client traffic and requires issuing database credentials to managers, both of which conflict with the stated constraints. Only the federated query approach through BigQuery views meets all the requirements simultaneously.

questions.text: Your logistics company maintains its shipment tracking data in Amazon DynamoDB. Due to increasing data volumes and plans to integrate machine learning models that predict delivery times, the team has decided to migrate to Cloud Bigtable. The migration should minimize operational complexity, keep costs reasonable, and avoid extended periods of unavailability. You also need to ensure that records remain consistent between the two databases throughout the transition. What should you do?

options.text: Export DynamoDB tables to JSON files, upload them to Cloud Storage, and use the cbt command line tool to import the data into Bigtable
options.explanation:
The best approach is to create a managed Dataflow pipeline that leverages the DynamoDBIO and BigtableIO connectors to continuously replicate data between the two systems. Dataflow is a fully managed service, which means there is no infrastructure to provision or maintain, directly addressing the requirement to minimize operational complexity. Because the pipeline can run in streaming mode, new and updated records in DynamoDB are propagated to Bigtable with very low latency, keeping both systems synchronized and reducing the window of unavailability during the transition. The purpose-built connectors simplify development effort and reduce the risk of consistency issues that could arise from hand-rolled solutions. This approach also scales automatically as data volumes increase, aligning well with the growing dataset and upcoming ML workloads.
Exporting DynamoDB tables to JSON files and importing them with the cbt tool is a batch-oriented process that does not maintain ongoing consistency between the two databases and would likely require repeated manual exports or scheduled downtime. Developing a custom application that directly uses both database SDKs significantly increases engineering effort, introduces maintenance burden, and raises the risk of partial failures or data inconsistencies during replication. Using a Dataproc cluster adds the overhead of managing cluster lifecycle, configuration, and scaling, and it is better suited to large batch processing jobs rather than continuous low-latency synchronization. None of these alternatives provide the combination of near real time replication, minimal operational overhead, and built-in fault tolerance that a Dataflow streaming pipeline offers.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company stores patient monitoring data in a Bigtable instance that powers a real-time alerting system. The system primarily queries data from the most recent 48 hours to detect anomalies in patient vitals. However, compliance with healthcare regulations requires the company to retain all monitoring data for a minimum of seven years. The Bigtable cluster is running low on storage capacity, and the engineering team must find a way to satisfy the retention requirement while keeping costs under control and maintaining low-latency performance on recent data. What is the best approach to address this situation?

options.text: Create a separate Bigtable instance dedicated to storing historical patient monitoring data
options.explanation:
The most appropriate solution is to offload aged data to Cloud Storage while deleting it from Bigtable. Cloud Storage costs significantly less per gigabyte than Bigtable SSD or HDD storage, making it well suited to multi-year retention needs. Since the alerting system only queries the last 48 hours of data, keeping years of historical records in Bigtable wastes expensive database resources. A common pattern involves scheduling a Dataflow job to periodically export rows beyond a certain age threshold to Cloud Storage and then purge them from Bigtable. This keeps the cluster lean and ensures that real-time query performance remains unaffected. The exported data remains accessible in Cloud Storage if it is ever needed to satisfy an audit or regulatory inquiry.
Increasing the node count does not solve the storage cost problem because Bigtable storage is billed based on the total gigabytes stored, independent of how many nodes are in the cluster. Nodes primarily affect processing throughput, not disk limits, so adding nodes would raise costs without addressing the underlying retention challenge. Enabling GZIP compression manually is not a valid configuration option because Bigtable already applies compression automatically at the storage layer, meaning there is no additional savings to gain from this approach. Moving historical data to a different Bigtable instance still incurs the same per-gigabyte storage costs as the original instance and does not provide the dramatic cost reduction that an archival storage service offers.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL Enterprise Plus instance that stores patient treatment records. Due to regulatory requirements, the database operations team must be able to restore the database to any precise moment within the past 30 days. What is the correct way to configure the Cloud SQL instance to satisfy this requirement?

options.text: Enable point-in-time recovery and set the transactionLogRetentionDays configuration setting to 30
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to enable point-in-time recovery with the transaction log retention period explicitly set to 30 days. Point-in-time recovery in Cloud SQL relies on binary logs that capture every change made to the database, allowing restoration to any specific second within the retention window. By default, Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus retains these transaction logs for seven days, so the retention must be explicitly increased to 30 days to meet the regulatory requirement. This configuration ensures the continuous stream of database changes is preserved long enough to allow the operations team to recover to any arbitrary timestamp within the compliance window. The combination of point-in-time recovery and the correct log retention setting is the only way to achieve second-level granularity over a 30-day period.
Scheduling frequent backups and extending the retention policy still only provides discrete snapshots of the database at fixed intervals, not the ability to restore to any arbitrary second. A failover replica provides high availability and automatic failover during outages, but it does not address the need to recover historical data to a specific moment in the past. Enabling binary logging is technically a prerequisite for point-in-time recovery, but adjusting undo log flags does not control how long Cloud SQL retains the binary logs needed for precise restoration. Without explicitly configuring the transaction log retention period, none of these alternatives can deliver the second-level recovery precision over a full 30-day window that the regulation demands.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a Cloud SQL database that powers a critical patient reporting dashboard. The system experiences sudden, unpredictable surges in query volume during public health events. Leadership has mandated that the database infrastructure must withstand a full regional failure, and the disaster recovery team needs a way to validate the failover workflow in advance without disrupting live operations. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Configure an external replica in a separate VPC and test instance migration
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to set up a cross-region read replica and verify the promotion process. A cross-region read replica maintains a continuously updated copy of the primary database in a geographically separate region, which directly addresses the requirement to survive a complete regional outage. Promoting that replica converts it into a fully independent primary instance capable of serving the entire application workload. Because the original primary database is unaffected by the promotion test, the team can validate the end-to-end recovery path without risking disruption to the production environment. This method also gives the team confidence that their RPO and RTO targets can be met when a real disaster occurs, since they are exercising the exact mechanism they would use in an emergency.
Establishing a zonal standby instance only protects against zone-level failures within the same region, which falls short of the requirement to survive a regional outage entirely. Point-in-time recovery is useful when dealing with accidental data corruption or deletion but does not maintain availability during an infrastructure disaster. Configuring an external replica in a separate VPC introduces significant networking complexity that would slow down restoration during an actual emergency, and testing instance migration does not directly validate a streamlined failover path. Deploying a cross-region failover replica and testing database cloning conflates two separate operations, since cloning creates a copy of the database at a point in time rather than confirming that a replica can seamlessly take over as the new primary. None of these alternatives provide the same integrated and verifiable disaster recovery workflow that promoting a cross-region read replica offers.

questions.text: A large healthcare analytics firm needs to replicate data from its on-premises PostgreSQL patient records system into BigQuery to power near real-time clinical reporting dashboards. The architecture must reduce operational overhead and deliver data to BigQuery with minimal delay. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: Deploy Cloud Data Fusion with a JDBC connector to extract records on a frequent schedule and write them into the data warehouse
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use Datastream combined with Dataflow to create a managed, low-latency CDC pipeline from the on-premises PostgreSQL database into BigQuery. Datastream is purpose-built to capture row-level changes from relational databases like PostgreSQL and stream them into Google Cloud. By landing those change events in Cloud Storage, Dataflow can then read and apply the inserts, updates, and deletes directly to BigQuery tables. This architecture is fully managed, scales automatically, and provides the near real-time data availability that clinical reporting dashboards demand without requiring the team to build or maintain custom extraction logic.
Using Database Migration Service alongside Cloud SQL introduces an unnecessary intermediate database layer, and querying that data through BigQuery external datasets relies on federated queries that typically perform worse than querying data stored natively in BigQuery. A custom application that exports CSV files and runs periodic BigQuery Load jobs is inherently batch-oriented and fragile, making it unsuitable when minimal latency is a requirement. Cloud Data Fusion with a JDBC connector operates on a batch or micro-batch schedule, which adds more latency and management complexity compared to a dedicated change data capture stream. None of these alternatives match the combination of low operational overhead and near real-time delivery that Datastream and Dataflow provide together.

Each database exceeds 4 TB in size and supports thousands of concurrent read queries from field devices and dashboards. What approach should you recommend?

options.text: Use the spanner-migration-tool to transform the schema into Cloud Spanner
options.explanation:
The recommended approach here is to leverage Database Migration Service alongside the Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus tier. Database Migration Service is purpose-built to handle MySQL migrations with minimal downtime by using native replication to keep the source and destination databases synchronized until you perform the cutover. Because Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus runs the MySQL 8.0 engine natively, the logistics company can preserve its existing schema, application code, and query patterns without modification. This combination delivers both a smooth migration path and the performance characteristics needed to handle multi-terabyte, read-intensive workloads in a mission-critical environment.

questions.text: A nationwide e-commerce company uses AlloyDB for PostgreSQL to power their product catalog and order analytics platform. During major sales events like Black Friday and holiday promotions, the volume of read-heavy analytics queries increases dramatically. The engineering team needs to design an architecture that handles these temporary spikes in read traffic without overspending during quieter periods. What approach should the team take to balance performance and cost?

options.text: Vertically resize the machine type of each node in a fixed-size read pool when performance degrades
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to begin with a minimal read pool and dynamically adjust the number of nodes as traffic fluctuates. AlloyDB read pools consist of one or more nodes that handle read-only queries, and they can be scaled horizontally by adding or removing nodes. Starting small and scaling based on actual demand means the company only pays for the compute resources it truly needs, which is critical when traffic spikes are temporary and seasonal. This elasticity also protects the primary instance from being burdened with heavy analytical workloads, preserving its capacity for writes and transactional operations. Horizontal scaling of read pool nodes is a natural fit for unpredictable or bursty read patterns commonly seen during promotional events.
Keeping the read pool at maximum size at all times wastes money during the majority of the year when traffic is normal. Directing analytics queries to the primary instance during peak periods is counterproductive because it risks degrading write performance and overall transactional responsiveness. Vertically resizing machine types across a static pool is more disruptive than simply adding or removing nodes, and it lacks the fine-grained control that horizontal scaling provides. A static configuration of any kind fails to take advantage of the elastic nature of cloud infrastructure, which is exactly what makes AlloyDB read pools well suited to workloads with variable demand.

questions.text: A startup uses Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL to power a real-time analytics pipeline. To maximize throughput on several large staging tables, the engineering team configured those tables as unlogged. When the team provisions a cloned instance through the Google Cloud Console to support a compliance review, they notice the staging tables exist but contain zero rows. What should the team do to provide the compliance reviewers with a complete copy of the data?

options.text: Export the source database and edit the dump file to include ALTER TABLE statements that convert all unlogged tables to logged before importing into the new instance
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to export the database, alter the unlogged table definitions to make them logged, and then import that modified dump into the target instance. Unlogged tables in PostgreSQL skip the write-ahead log entirely, which gives them better write performance but makes their data invisible to any process that depends on WAL-based replication. Cloud SQL cloning and automated backups both rely on the WAL to capture table data, so any table that bypasses it will appear as an empty structure in the resulting clone. By converting those tables to logged in the export file and performing a manual import, you force the data into a durable, portable format that the new instance can fully consume.
Enabling synchronous commit changes how the database confirms transactions to the client but has no effect on whether a table writes to the WAL in the first place. That setting governs durability guarantees at the transaction level, not the logging property of individual tables. Point-in-time recovery also depends on the WAL stream, so it will reproduce the same empty tables because the underlying data was never recorded in those logs. Adjusting IAM roles such as cloudsql.editor addresses permission issues, but permissions are not the problem here since the table schemas are visible and only the row data is absent. Adding more storage and re-running the clone does not change the fundamental behavior of how unlogged tables interact with the cloning mechanism and will produce identical empty results.

questions.text: Your team maintains a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance that powers a monthly financial reconciliation dashboard. The database receives a bulk data load at the beginning of each month from files stored in Cloud Storage, and analysts run read-only queries against it during the remaining weeks. Due to corporate policy, all database data and backups must remain within a single designated region. You want to keep infrastructure costs as low as possible while still being able to recover the database within 24 hours if something goes wrong. What should you do?

options.text: Disable high availability and set up a read replica in the same region as the primary instance
options.explanation:
The most cost-effective approach here is to turn off high availability and specify a regional location when configuring automated backups. High availability on Cloud SQL provisions a standby instance in a separate zone, which essentially doubles compute costs. For a database that is bulk-loaded once a month and only serves read-only dashboard queries, this level of protection is excessive. A 24-hour recovery window is well within what automated backups can deliver, since restoring from a backup typically takes far less time than that. Storing the backups in a regional bucket satisfies the corporate policy requiring all data to stay within a single region.
Enabling high availability would add unnecessary cost because the workload is not mission-critical and does not require near-zero downtime. The default multi-regional backup location would also violate the policy that restricts data to a single region. Deploying a cross-region replica directly contradicts the data residency requirement by placing a copy of the data outside the designated region. Creating a read replica within the same region adds compute and storage expenses that are not justified, since the analyst query load does not demand the kind of read scaling that replicas provide. The simple combination of automated backups stored regionally gives you adequate disaster recovery at the lowest possible cost.

questions.text: Your company runs a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance that supports an internal order management system. Over the weekend, several automated batch jobs failed silently, and the operations team only discovered the failures on Monday morning when processing delays were reported by the warehouse. After investigating, you find repeated "out of connections" errors in the database logs spanning from Friday evening through Sunday. Management wants to ensure the team is immediately aware of similar connection exhaustion events going forward rather than learning about them days later. What should you do?

options.text: Build a Cloud Monitoring dashboard displaying connection pool metrics that could indicate exhaustion
options.explanation:
The best approach is to create a log-based metric that captures every occurrence of the specific error string and then attach an alerting policy that notifies the team immediately. Cloud Monitoring can watch log entries in near real time, so as soon as the "out of connections" error appears, the metric increments and the alert fires. This means the operations team receives a notification through email, SMS, or another channel within moments of the problem starting. The solution uses native Google Cloud capabilities designed specifically to turn log data into actionable signals without requiring any custom code or scheduled processes. It directly addresses management's requirement that the team be informed right away rather than after a multi-day delay.
Exporting logs to BigQuery on a nightly schedule still introduces up to a full day of delay between when the error occurs and when someone discovers it, which does not satisfy the need for immediate awareness. A Cloud Monitoring dashboard showing connection pool metrics would require someone to be actively watching the dashboard at the exact time the problem occurs, and connection pool metrics alone may not clearly surface the specific error condition without manual interpretation. Deploying a Cloud Function on a 30-minute polling interval adds infrastructure complexity, ongoing maintenance burden, and cost that are unnecessary when log-based metrics handle this natively. A 30-minute gap also means the team could remain unaware of a critical failure for half an hour, and the function itself could fail without notice, creating a secondary reliability concern.

questions.text: A logistics company stores shipment tracking data in Cloud Bigtable, with thousands of sensors feeding location updates every second. The system handles global queries well, but a specific automated reporting job that pulls data for a single distribution center consistently times out every morning at 8:00 AM local time. The operations team suspects the issue is related to how the data is organized rather than overall system capacity. What is the best first step to diagnose why this particular query pattern experiences degraded performance?

options.text: Analyze the heatmaps in the Key Visualizer
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use the Key Visualizer heatmaps to understand how read and write traffic is distributed across the row key space. Since the timeout is tied to a specific query pattern hitting a single distribution center at a predictable time, the most likely cause is a hot-spotting problem where all the relevant rows are clustered on the same tablet. The Key Visualizer provides a time-based visual representation of access patterns, showing you exactly which row key ranges are under heavy pressure during the morning reporting window. This allows you to confirm whether the distribution center's data is concentrated in a narrow key range, which would explain why that particular access pattern degrades while everything else remains healthy. Once you can see the uneven distribution, you can make an informed decision about whether to restructure the row key schema to spread the load more evenly.
Changing the garbage collection policy controls how old cell versions are cleaned up and has no bearing on why active queries against current data are timing out. Spinning up a separate instance adds infrastructure cost and management overhead without addressing the underlying schema design problem that is causing the bottleneck. Reviewing total storage utilization tells you about disk consumption across the cluster but reveals nothing about traffic concentration or which specific key ranges are creating pressure during the problematic time window. None of these alternatives provide the granular, time-correlated visibility into access patterns that is needed to pinpoint a hot-spotting issue.

questions.text: A multinational insurance company is migrating its policy management platform from on-premises infrastructure to Google Cloud. The application makes extensive use of PostgreSQL-specific features including custom functions, triggers, and advanced indexing. The platform must achieve a Recovery Time Objective under 10 minutes and near-zero data loss in the event of a zonal outage. Regulatory constraints require that all transactional writes remain within a single designated country, but claims adjusters and compliance teams spread across Asia, Europe, and North America need fast read access to policy data. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: A multi-region Cloud SQL PostgreSQL deployment using internal load balancing to distribute write traffic globally
options.explanation:
An AlloyDB cluster with high availability and cross-region read replicas is the best fit here. AlloyDB is fully compatible with PostgreSQL, which means the company can migrate its custom functions, triggers, and advanced indexing without rewriting application logic. The high-availability configuration in AlloyDB separates compute from storage, enabling rapid automated failover that comfortably meets the sub-10-minute RTO and near-zero RPO targets. Because all writes remain within the primary region, the regulatory requirement to keep transactional data in a specific country is satisfied. Cross-region read replicas then allow claims adjusters and compliance teams on other continents to query policy data with low latency.
A Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance with high availability and regional storage handles zonal failures well within a single region, but it lacks the ability to serve low-latency reads to users on different continents since it does not offer cross-region read replicas in this configuration. A multi-region Cloud Spanner deployment would provide global read access and strong consistency, but adopting Spanner would demand a substantial rewrite of the PostgreSQL-specific features the application depends on, including triggers and custom functions. The option involving multi-region Cloud SQL with internal load balancing to distribute writes globally directly violates the regulatory mandate that all write operations must stay within a single jurisdiction, making it unsuitable regardless of its other characteristics.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that stores patient billing records updated continuously throughout the day. A recent incident where a batch job accidentally overwrote critical records has prompted the team to implement a recovery strategy. The operations team needs the ability to restore the database to any specific second within the past 7 days to undo erroneous changes without losing subsequent valid transactions. What should you do?

options.text: Create a cross-region read replica to maintain a secondary copy
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to enable both automated backups and binary logging on the Cloud SQL instance. Point-in-time recovery requires two components working together: a base snapshot from an automated backup and a continuous record of every database change captured through binary logs. When you request a restoration to a precise second, Cloud SQL first restores the most recent automated backup taken before that timestamp and then replays the binary log entries up to the exact moment you specify. This mechanism allows the team to roll the database back to the second before the erroneous batch job executed, preserving all legitimate transactions that occurred prior to the mistake. Without binary logging enabled alongside automated backups, the granular log-replay capability that makes point-in-time recovery possible simply does not exist.
Setting a retention window on scheduled backups alone only lets you restore to the exact times when those backups were captured, not to arbitrary points between them. If the faulty batch job ran three hours after the last backup, those three hours of valid data would be unrecoverable. A cross-region read replica provides geographic redundancy and can offload read traffic, but it mirrors data changes in near real-time, meaning the corrupted records would propagate to the replica almost immediately. A high-availability instance with a standby in another zone protects against infrastructure failures like a zonal outage, but it offers no defense against logical data corruption because the standby receives and applies the same harmful writes as the primary.

questions.text: A financial services company uses AlloyDB for PostgreSQL to power its online trading application. The primary instance processes all incoming trade orders and account updates throughout the day. The risk management team needs to run heavy aggregation queries against the production data every few hours to generate compliance reports, but these queries are degrading the latency of live trade processing. Management wants to isolate the reporting workload from production traffic while minimizing costs overnight and on weekends when no compliance reports are generated. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Deploy a primary instance alongside a large fixed-size read pool instance dedicated to compliance reporting
options.explanation:
The best solution is to pair the primary instance with a read pool that automatically adjusts its capacity based on observed CPU load. AlloyDB supports autoscaling on read pool instances, and by setting a 75% CPU utilization target, the system will add or remove read pool nodes as demand fluctuates. When the risk management team is running heavy aggregation queries during the day, the read pool scales out to handle the load. During nights and weekends when no reporting is happening, the pool contracts back to its minimum size, which keeps costs low. The primary instance stays focused on trade processing, so there is no contention between the two workloads.
Running both workloads on a single primary instance would directly cause the latency issues the company is already experiencing, since analytical queries compete with transactional operations for the same compute resources. Provisioning a large fixed-size read pool wastes money during off-hours and weekends when there is no reporting activity, making it a poor fit when cost efficiency is a stated goal. Manually scaling the read pool up and down based on a schedule might seem reasonable, but it cannot react to unexpected surges in query volume or days when reporting finishes early. Only the CPU-based autoscaling policy provides both cost efficiency and the ability to respond dynamically to real workload patterns without requiring human intervention.

After onboarding several new hospital clients, the database has grown rapidly and storage consumption has reached 80% of the allowed maximum. The operations team is concerned that continued data growth will soon prevent new writes from being accepted. options.text: Reconfigure the instance to use a multi-regional deployment topology
options.explanation:

This change takes effect almost immediately and does not require downtime, making it the right approach during an urgent growth scenario. It is the standard mechanism recommended by Google Cloud to address storage limits in Spanner.

Switching to a multi-regional deployment changes how data is replicated across regions to improve global availability and latency, but it does not alter the fundamental relationship between compute capacity and storage limits. Adding a read-only replica in another zone improves fault tolerance and read scalability, but it does not expand the storage quota available to the primary instance. questions.text: A logistics company has been running a continuous migration from their self-managed PostgreSQL instance to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL using the Database Migration Service. The migration job has been active and replicating changes successfully for several days. A development team has just deployed an application update that introduced a new inventory tracking table on the source database. The team needs this table to be part of the ongoing replication stream without disrupting the existing migration or causing data inconsistencies across the already-migrated tables. What is the correct approach to include this newly created table in the active replication?

options.text: Navigate to the Database Migration Service settings in the Cloud Console and modify the job to include the additional table
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use the pglogical extension directly on the source database to add the new table to the active replication set. Database Migration Service relies on the pglogical extension as its underlying replication mechanism when performing continuous migrations from PostgreSQL. When a new table is created on the source after the migration job has already started, it is not automatically detected or included in the replication stream. By running pglogical.replication_set_add_table, you explicitly register the table with the replication set that the migration job is already consuming, which allows the new table's data and subsequent changes to flow through the existing pipeline seamlessly.
Stopping the migration job and starting over would waste significant time and resources by re-dumping and re-loading all the data that has already been successfully migrated, and it would extend the overall migration window unnecessarily. The Database Migration Service interface in the Cloud Console does not expose a mechanism to dynamically add individual tables to a running migration job, so attempting to modify the configuration there would not work. Manually exporting and importing just the new table with pg_dump and restore would place that table outside the logical replication stream, meaning any future inserts or updates to that table on the source would not be captured, creating a divergence between source and destination that undermines the purpose of continuous replication.

questions.text: A retail company uses Google Cloud Memorystore for Redis to cache product catalog data and user shopping cart sessions. During a seasonal sales event, the engineering team notices degraded response times across the application. Upon reviewing Cloud Monitoring dashboards, they observe a significant spike in evicted keys metrics while the number of client connections has remained consistent throughout the event. The team needs to address the root cause of the performance degradation. What should they do?

options.text: Increase the memory size of the Memorystore instance
options.explanation:
Increasing the memory allocated to the Memorystore instance directly resolves the underlying problem. The evicted keys metric confirms that Redis is hitting its maximum memory threshold and is forced to remove existing entries to make room for new writes. This eviction process introduces additional processing overhead that manifests as latency spikes in the application. By scaling up the instance memory, the full working dataset can reside in cache without triggering the eviction policy, which restores a high cache hit ratio and eliminates the performance penalty caused by constant key removal.
Extending the expiration duration on cached keys would make the situation worse because data would persist longer in an already constrained memory space, accelerating the rate at which evictions occur. Redeploying application servers to the same region as the Memorystore instance could reduce network round-trip time, but the monitoring data clearly points to memory pressure rather than network latency as the source of the problem. Adding read replicas helps distribute query load across multiple nodes, which is useful when connection volume or read throughput is the bottleneck. However, read replicas do not expand the memory capacity of the primary instance, so evictions would continue at the same rate regardless of how many replicas are added.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup runs a cluster of containerized Go and Python services on Google Kubernetes Engine. These services query an AlloyDB for PostgreSQL instance that holds patient billing data. After a recent security audit, the team has been told that no static database passwords may exist anywhere in the system, including in Kubernetes Secrets, configuration files, or hardcoded connection strings. You must redesign the database authentication method to satisfy this requirement while maintaining connectivity from all microservices. What should you do?

options.text: Configure each microservice to authenticate by passing the bound service account email as the database username during connection
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to leverage the AlloyDB language connector with IAM-based database authentication, which replaces traditional password credentials with automatically generated short-lived OAuth 2.0 tokens. When this mode is enabled, the connector running inside each microservice obtains a token from the metadata server using the identity of the Kubernetes workload's attached service account. Because tokens expire quickly and are never stored on disk, there is no static secret that could be leaked or extracted from a configuration file. The entire token lifecycle is managed transparently by the connector library, so developers do not need to write custom authentication logic.
Routing traffic through Identity-Aware Proxy can help reach a private AlloyDB endpoint, but it only handles network-level access and still requires the application to present valid database credentials when opening a session. Enabling SSL on the AlloyDB instance encrypts data as it travels between client and server, yet SSL enforcement alone does nothing to eliminate the need for a username and password at login time, and storing a service account key locally is itself a static credential. Passing the service account email as the database username without using the connector's token exchange simply sends an identifier with no accompanying proof of identity, or it still relies on a password or key file to complete the handshake, neither of which meets the audit requirement to remove all static secrets from the system.

questions.text: A healthcare technology startup deploys its patient portal application on Google Kubernetes Engine. The application connects to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that stores appointment and billing records. A recent compliance review determined that all database passwords must be automatically rotated every 60 days, and the application must seamlessly retrieve the latest valid credentials without any downtime or manual updates. You want to implement this requirement with minimal operational overhead. What should you do?

options.text: Encrypt the database password with a Cloud KMS key and embed the encrypted value directly into the application's container image
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use Secret Manager with an automated rotation schedule driven by a Cloud Function. Secret Manager provides a centralized, audited location to store sensitive credentials, and its built-in rotation functionality allows you to trigger a Cloud Function every 60 days. That Cloud Function can update the password in both Cloud SQL and Secret Manager simultaneously, ensuring the application always retrieves a valid credential through a simple API call. This eliminates manual intervention, avoids hardcoded values, and directly satisfies the compliance requirement to rotate database passwords on a defined schedule.
Encrypting a password with Cloud KMS and embedding it inside a container image makes rotation extremely difficult because you would need to rebuild and redeploy containers across the entire cluster every time the password changes. Storing credentials in a Cloud Storage configuration file lacks any native mechanism to automate password rotation on a schedule, leaving you to build and maintain custom tooling while increasing the risk of stale credentials. IAM database authentication uses short-lived tokens rather than traditional passwords, which means it does not actually fulfill the stated compliance requirement to rotate a database password every 60 days. Additionally, the option describes mapping a separate database user per patient account, which is not a standard or practical pattern and would create significant management complexity.

questions.text: A nationwide logistics company is migrating its shipment tracking platform to Google Cloud Spanner to support millions of daily package scans across distribution centers. The engineering team has discovered that the existing schema uses an auto-incrementing integer as the primary key for the shipment records table. During peak holiday shipping periods, the system must handle extremely high write throughput. The team wants to prevent write concentration on a single node while keeping the primary key as a numeric data type rather than converting to strings or byte arrays. Which strategy should the engineering team adopt?

options.text: Implement an application-level counter that tracks and increments based on the most recent record
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to configure the primary key to draw from a bit-reversed positive sequence, which Cloud Spanner natively supports. Cloud Spanner distributes data across splits based on the lexicographic ordering of primary keys. When a strictly increasing integer is used, all new inserts cluster at the tail end of the keyspace, meaning a single node handles the bulk of write traffic. Bit-reversal takes a monotonically increasing number and flips its binary representation, which scatters the resulting values across a wide range of the keyspace. This ensures that write operations are distributed evenly across multiple Spanner nodes, enabling the system to scale horizontally during peak periods. Crucially, bit-reversed sequences preserve the integer data type, so the team avoids a costly schema migration away from numeric primary keys.

questions.text: A rapidly growing e-commerce platform is preparing for a flash sale event where millions of customers will simultaneously place orders, update carts, and process payments. The engineering team anticipates highly variable and bursty write traffic to their Cloud Spanner database during the event window. They need the database to automatically adapt to these unpredictable load patterns while keeping transaction latency consistently low and avoiding runaway infrastructure costs. What should the team configure?

options.text: Configure Cloud Spanner autoscaling with a defined maximum processing unit or node limit
options.explanation:
Cloud Spanner offers a built-in autoscaling capability that dynamically adjusts the number of processing units or nodes based on real-time CPU utilization and storage thresholds. This is the ideal approach when write traffic is unpredictable because the system scales up automatically during demand spikes and scales back down when traffic subsides. Setting a maximum limit ensures that costs remain bounded even during extreme traffic surges, giving the team both performance elasticity and financial control without any manual intervention.
Provisioning read-only replicas helps distribute read queries but does not increase the write throughput of the underlying Spanner instance, which is the primary concern during a flash sale with heavy transactional writes. Deploying multiple Spanner instances across regions and manually splitting tables introduces significant operational complexity and eliminates the advantages of Spanner as a single globally consistent database. Using sequentially incrementing primary keys is actually an anti-pattern in Cloud Spanner because it causes write hotspots, where all new rows are directed to the same server split, leading to severe performance degradation under load. The correct approach leverages the native autoscaling mechanism to handle bursty write workloads dynamically.

questions.text: A multinational retail corporation is planning to move its Oracle-based inventory management system to AlloyDB on Google Cloud. The existing database relies heavily on PL/SQL stored procedures, materialized views, and complex triggers that must be converted to PostgreSQL syntax. The system supports real-time operations across dozens of distribution centers worldwide, so extended periods of unavailability during the migration are not acceptable. Which migration strategy should the team implement?

options.text: Perform schema translation with ora2pg and handle data movement through a self-managed ora2pg process
options.explanation:
The best strategy here is to use the Database Migration Service conversion workspace alongside a continuous migration job. The conversion workspace is the current, fully integrated approach within Google Cloud that automates the translation of Oracle-specific constructs like PL/SQL stored procedures, materialized views, and triggers into PostgreSQL-compatible equivalents. This native tooling provides sophisticated schema mapping and is purpose-built to work with AlloyDB as a destination. A continuous migration job uses change data capture to keep the target database synchronized with the source in near real time, which means the actual cutover window can be reduced to seconds. This combination delivers a unified, managed workflow that handles both structural conversion and ongoing data replication within one platform.
Using ora2pg to manage both schema conversion and data migration introduces significant manual effort and lacks the managed automation and monitoring capabilities that enterprise-scale migrations demand. While ora2pg is a respected open-source tool, it does not provide the same level of integration with AlloyDB or the streamlined experience of a cloud-native service. The legacy conversion tool within Database Migration Service has been superseded by the modern conversion workspace, which offers deeper mapping capabilities and better compatibility with current AlloyDB features. Converting the schema with ora2pg and then running a one-time migration job would require the source database to remain offline throughout the entire data copy, resulting in prolonged downtime that directly conflicts with the requirement to keep distribution centers operational during the transition.

questions.text: A financial services company is migrating its on-premises Oracle database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL using Database Migration Service. The migration job has been configured and started successfully. During validation, the database team observes that most tables are replicating correctly, but one critical transactions table with millions of rows shows zero rows migrated to the destination. The table schema was transferred without errors. Network logs confirm no connectivity issues, and the migration service account has full read access to the source database. What should the team do to resolve this table-level replication failure?

options.text: Remove any NCLOB columns from the table definition
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to ensure the table has a primary key defined. Database Migration Service uses logical replication to continuously capture and apply changes from the source to the destination. Logical replication requires a unique row identifier to track inserts, updates, and deletes accurately. When a table lacks a primary key, the replication engine has no reliable way to distinguish one row from another, which causes the service to skip that table entirely during data synchronization. This explains why the schema was created successfully but no actual row data was transferred. Adding a primary key gives the migration service the row-level identity it needs to include the table in the replication stream and maintain data consistency throughout the migration.
Enabling supplemental logging ensures that additional column data is written to Oracle redo logs, which can be useful for certain replication configurations, but it does not address the fundamental problem of missing unique row identification. Converting from index-organized to heap-organized storage changes the physical arrangement of data on disk, which has no bearing on whether logical replication can identify and track individual rows. Removing NCLOB columns might resolve specific data type compatibility warnings or performance concerns during migration, but large object columns are not the reason a table would be completely excluded from replication. The only action that directly resolves a table being silently skipped by the replication process is providing a mechanism to uniquely identify each row.
