questions.text: A financial technology company runs a Cloud Bigtable instance that powers a real-time fraud detection dashboard. The system was originally designed to handle transaction data from a single country, but the company recently integrated merchant data from several additional countries. After this integration, the operations team noticed that certain queries have become significantly slower and that tablet server utilization is heavily skewed, with some servers handling far more traffic than others. The team needs to resolve these performance problems while keeping the architecture simple and manageable. What should you do? (Select two)

options.text: Redesign row keys and column family layout to ensure more even data distribution across tablet servers
options.explanation:
The best approach here combines improving how data is physically distributed with controlling how requests reach the cluster. When new data patterns are introduced, such as merchant records from multiple countries, the original row key design may cause hotspotting where certain tablet servers receive a disproportionate share of reads and writes. Redesigning the row key structure and column family organization helps spread data more uniformly across all nodes, which directly addresses the skewed utilization the team observed. Additionally, setting up app profiles with single-cluster routing ensures that the newly integrated workload is consistently directed to one cluster, which reduces latency variability and prevents unpredictable cross-region request handling. Together, these two changes target both the data layer and the routing layer without adding significant operational overhead.
Reducing the node count removes available capacity and would likely make performance worse, not better, since the remaining nodes would need to absorb the same volume of traffic with fewer resources. Enabling multi-cluster routing across all regions can cause requests to be served by geographically distant clusters, which introduces higher latency and is counterproductive when workloads are region-specific. Archiving older data to Cloud Storage does not address the root cause of uneven load distribution or slow active queries, since the performance issue stems from how current data is structured and accessed rather than from overall dataset size.

options.text: AlloyDB with PostgreSQL compatibility

questions.text: A financial services company operates a Cloud Spanner database that handles thousands of real-time payment transactions per second. Their data science team needs to periodically run large aggregate queries across months of transaction history and join Spanner data with reference tables already stored in BigQuery. The engineering team is concerned that these analytical workloads will degrade latency on the payment processing pipeline by competing with production traffic. What is the best way to address this concern?

options.text: Scale up the Spanner instance node count before each analytical job begins
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Spanner Data Boost alongside BigQuery federated queries. Data Boost allocates an independent, serverless pool of compute resources that handles heavy read operations without drawing from the CPU or memory of the production Spanner instance. This means the payment processing workload continues to operate at normal latency while the data science team runs complex aggregations over large historical datasets. Because federated queries in BigQuery can target Spanner directly through Data Boost, the team can also join the results with their existing BigQuery reference tables in a single query. There is no need to provision or manage any additional infrastructure, and costs are incurred only when these analytical jobs actually execute.
Adding a secondary index may improve the speed of certain lookups, but it does not prevent the query execution itself from consuming the production nodes' resources. The read load from scanning months of transaction data would still compete with real-time payment processing. Increasing the node count before each analytical run adds capacity to the shared environment, but the analytical and transactional workloads still run on the same compute, which does not guarantee isolation. This approach also wastes money during periods when the extra nodes sit idle. Running a Dataflow pipeline with many workers can parallelize data extraction, but each worker still issues read requests against the main Spanner instance, placing significant demand on the production nodes. None of these alternatives provide the dedicated compute isolation that Data Boost delivers.

questions.text: A logistics company operates a fleet of thousands of delivery vehicles, each equipped with GPS and onboard diagnostics. Every few seconds, each vehicle transmits its location, speed, engine temperature, and fuel level to a central platform. Engineers need to query recent telemetry by vehicle ID across specific time windows to detect anomalies and optimize routes. The data volume doubles every quarter, and the architecture must expand seamlessly without requiring engineers to manually partition or redistribute data. Which Google Cloud database is the best fit?

options.text: Firestore
options.explanation:
Cloud Bigtable is the right choice here because it is purpose-built to handle massive, sustained write throughput and scales horizontally by simply adding nodes. Vehicle telemetry that arrives every few seconds from thousands of sources creates an append-heavy ingestion pattern, which is exactly where Bigtable excels. Its row-key design allows you to model data with a composite key of vehicle ID and timestamp, making range scans over time windows extremely efficient. As the data volume doubles each quarter, Bigtable can grow without any manual sharding or repartitioning, since it automatically redistributes data across its nodes. This makes it a natural match for high-volume time-series workloads with predictable access patterns.
Cloud SQL is a managed relational database that primarily scales vertically and would struggle to keep up with the sustained ingestion rate described in this scenario. Cloud Spanner provides global distribution and strong transactional consistency, but those capabilities add complexity and cost that are unnecessary when the workload is simple key-based lookups and range scans rather than complex relational queries. Firestore is a document-oriented database well suited to mobile and web application data with flexible schemas, but it is not designed to handle the continuous, high-throughput sequential writes that vehicle telemetry demands. Any of these services could technically store timestamped records, but none of them match the performance and scalability profile that this particular workload requires.

questions.text: Your company runs a Cloud Bigtable instance that powers a customer-facing recommendation engine with strict latency requirements. The data science team wants to run large historical data scans against the same dataset to train machine learning models. These scan operations are resource-intensive and will run on a scheduled basis throughout the day. You need to prevent these heavy read operations from degrading the recommendation engine's response times. What should you do?

options.text: Scale up the node count on the existing cluster and configure autoscaling to handle the increased demand
options.explanation:
-
The right approach here is to leverage Cloud Bigtable's multi-cluster architecture combined with application profiles to achieve workload isolation. When you add a second cluster to the same instance, Bigtable automatically replicates the data across both clusters. You can then create a dedicated app profile that routes all data science scan traffic exclusively to the new cluster, while the recommendation engine continues to use the original cluster undisturbed. Application profiles give you granular control over which cluster handles which type of traffic, making this the standard pattern for separating batch and real-time workloads within the same Bigtable instance.
Spinning up an entirely separate instance and using Dataflow to replicate data introduces unnecessary operational complexity and data synchronization challenges when the same result can be achieved natively within a single instance. Changing the default app profile to multi-cluster routing would spread traffic across all clusters without any guarantee of isolation, meaning the heavy scan operations could still land on the same cluster serving the recommendation engine. Increasing the node count and enabling autoscaling improves raw capacity but does nothing to separate the two workloads at a resource level. A burst of scan activity could still consume disproportionate resources on a shared cluster, and autoscaling may not respond quickly enough to prevent latency spikes during sudden increases in batch processing load.

questions.text: A healthcare organization runs multiple Cloud SQL databases containing patient records. Due to regulatory requirements, all database backups must remain within a specific geographic region. The organization needs automated daily backups to support routine recovery scenarios. Additionally, they require monthly full backups that must be retained indefinitely until each respective database instance is permanently deleted. What is the best strategy to meet all of these requirements?

options.text: Implement an Organization Policy to restrict resource locations and configure automated backup retention to 30 days
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to combine Cloud SQL automated backups with manually triggered on-demand backups taken each month and stored in a specific location. Automated backups handle the daily recovery requirement reliably and run on a predictable schedule managed by Cloud SQL. However, automated backups are subject to a configurable retention window and will eventually be deleted once that window passes. On-demand backups in Cloud SQL do not expire automatically, which means they persist until you explicitly delete them or until the instance itself is removed. This makes on-demand backups the right tool when you need monthly snapshots that survive the entire lifecycle of a database instance. On-demand backups also allow you to specify a custom storage location, which directly addresses the geographic residency constraint imposed by healthcare regulations.
Setting the automated backup retention period to 365 days would still result in those backups being purged after one year, which fails the requirement that monthly backups persist until the instance is decommissioned. Scheduling both daily and monthly operations as on-demand backups is not a native capability within Cloud SQL without relying on external automation, and it would also mean giving up the simplicity and built-in reliability of automated daily backups. Applying an Organization Policy with a 30-day automated backup retention addresses resource placement broadly but does nothing to create monthly full backups or ensure indefinite retention of those snapshots. Organization Policies control where resources can be provisioned but they do not manage backup scheduling or retention logic within Cloud SQL.

questions.text: A national healthcare analytics firm stores patient visit records and real-time appointment scheduling data in a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database with 16 vCPUs and 64 GB of RAM. Data scientists run complex aggregation queries across tens of millions of records to identify treatment outcome patterns and hospital utilization trends. These queries regularly push CPU usage above 85% and cause noticeable latency in the appointment booking system that serves hundreds of clinics. The database simultaneously handles a large number of concurrent inserts from electronic health record integrations and read-heavy traffic from clinic-facing dashboards. The firm plans to onboard several new hospital networks and needs to ensure that analytical workloads do not degrade the responsiveness of the transactional appointment system while also gaining elastic read capacity and faster processing of large-scale statistical scans. What architecture should you implement?

options.text: Migrate to AlloyDB with a 16 vCPU primary instance, columnar engine enabled, and dedicated read pools
options.explanation:
AlloyDB is the right solution here because it was built to handle hybrid transactional and analytical processing (HTAP) workloads where both types of traffic share the same database. The columnar engine maintains a memory-resident columnar format that dramatically speeds up the aggregation queries and statistical scans the data scientists are running across tens of millions of patient records. Read pools allow the firm to horizontally and elastically scale read capacity so that analytical queries run on separate compute resources, completely isolating them from the primary instance that handles appointment scheduling writes and clinic dashboard reads. This combination directly addresses every requirement in the scenario: workload isolation, analytical acceleration, and elastic horizontal scaling.
Upgrading to a 32 vCPU Cloud SQL instance with High Availability and PgBouncer addresses connection management and provides more raw compute, but it does not separate analytical workloads from transactional ones, meaning heavy queries will still compete with appointment writes on the same CPU and memory. Moving to Enterprise Plus Cloud SQL with read replicas does offload some read traffic, but those replicas use standard row-based storage and lack any specialized columnar processing, so complex aggregations across millions of rows will still be slow and resource-intensive. Keeping the current Cloud SQL instance and adding materialized views with more storage can help with a limited set of predefined reports, but materialized views are static and must be refreshed, they do not provide general-purpose elastic scaling, and increasing disk size does nothing to resolve CPU contention from analytical scans.

questions.text: A financial technology company is developing a fraud detection system that must evaluate transactions in real time with sub-millisecond latency. The system processes millions of events per second during peak shopping periods and must remain operational even if an entire availability zone goes offline. The engineering team has chosen Cloud Bigtable as the storage engine and needs to design the deployment to meet strict uptime requirements while also being able to absorb unpredictable traffic surges without operator involvement. How should you architect the Bigtable deployment?

options.text: Create a single Bigtable instance with a large number of nodes to maximize throughput and durability
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to provision a Bigtable instance that spans multiple zones using replicated clusters combined with ongoing performance monitoring. When you configure a Bigtable instance with clusters in different zones and use a multi-cluster routing policy, the system can automatically redirect traffic to a healthy cluster if one zone experiences a failure. Replication between the clusters keeps the data synchronized, which provides both redundancy and the ability to spread read traffic across zones. Adding performance monitoring ensures the team can track latency, throughput, and node utilization so they can verify the cluster is properly sized and respond proactively to any degradation.
Managing data distribution manually across separate Bigtable instances adds significant complexity to the application layer and makes it much harder to maintain consistency, since replication between independent instances is not handled automatically. Running a single cluster in one zone with an optimized client-side write buffer might help smooth out brief write bursts, but it leaves the entire system vulnerable to a zonal outage with no automatic failover path. Simply increasing the node count within a single cluster improves raw processing capacity, but it does nothing to protect against zone-level infrastructure failures because all nodes still reside in the same location. The question specifically calls out resilience against localized hardware failures and the ability to handle traffic spikes without manual intervention, and only a multi-zone replicated configuration with monitoring addresses both of those requirements together.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Google Cloud Bigtable cluster to support its fraud detection system, which processes millions of transactions per second. The platform reliably meets its sub-10-millisecond latency targets, but leadership has flagged that the compute costs from the large number of provisioned nodes are significantly over budget. You are tasked with lowering the operational expenses of this Bigtable deployment while maintaining the current level of performance and responsiveness. What should you do?

options.text: Switch the current SSD-based storage to HDD-based storage
options.explanation:
The most effective way to reduce Bigtable costs without sacrificing performance is to examine how the application reads and writes data, then adjust the schema accordingly. Poorly designed row keys can create hotspots where a small subset of nodes handles a disproportionate share of traffic while others sit underutilized. By analyzing access patterns, you can restructure keys to distribute load more evenly across all nodes in the cluster. Once requests are balanced, the same throughput can often be sustained with fewer nodes, which directly reduces the monthly compute bill. This approach addresses the underlying inefficiency rather than making trade-offs that compromise the system.
Reducing the replication factor would cut costs but would also weaken availability and resilience, which is particularly risky in a fraud detection system that must remain operational at all times. Moving from SSD to HDD storage would introduce higher read latencies that would break the sub-10-millisecond requirement the platform depends on. Merging multiple tables into a single wide table does not inherently improve how work is distributed across nodes and can actually complicate query patterns and schema management without yielding meaningful cost savings.

questions.text: A software engineering team running an e-commerce platform on AlloyDB for PostgreSQL notices that checkout latency has increased dramatically during peak hours. The team suspects that certain microservices or API endpoints are generating unusually expensive queries, but they cannot determine which ones without deeper visibility into how application-level context maps to database activity. Which tool or feature should they use to pinpoint which application routes and services are driving the most database resource consumption?

options.text: PostgreSQL pg_stat_activity
options.explanation:
### Explanation
AlloyDB Query Insights is the right tool here because it provides a built-in dashboard that surfaces top queries alongside application-level metadata such as tags and request routes. This allows the engineering team to see a direct breakdown of which microservices or API endpoints are contributing the most latency and consuming the most database resources. The feature captures wait events and execution statistics directly from the database engine, making it easy to identify bottlenecks tied to specific parts of the application without requiring custom instrumentation or manual data joining. It also provides historical aggregation, which is essential when investigating intermittent performance issues that only appear during peak traffic windows. The visual correlation between application context and database wait states makes root cause analysis straightforward.
Cloud Logging filters can surface event-level data and timestamps but do not offer the granular query performance metrics or application-level tagging needed to trace load back to specific services or endpoints. The Index Advisor is designed to recommend index changes that could speed up certain queries, but it does not help identify which application components are responsible for generating expensive workloads. PostgreSQL pg_stat_activity shows currently executing queries and their states, but it lacks historical trend data and does not associate queries with application-level attributes like route or service name. Relying on standard PostgreSQL catalog views would require significant manual effort to aggregate and correlate data across time and across multiple nodes in the cluster.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs its patient billing database on Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL 15 Enterprise Edition. Following a recent regulatory review, the data governance team has mandated that all production databases must support a 35-day point-in-time recovery capability to satisfy healthcare compliance requirements. The database administrators have reported that the current setup only supports a 7-day recovery window, and attempts to retain additional write-ahead logs on the instance disk are consuming significant storage space and degrading query performance during peak billing cycles. What is the most appropriate solution to achieve the 35-day point-in-time recovery requirement while preventing recovery logs from impacting the primary disk?

options.text: Increase the persistent disk allocation to store additional WAL files
options.explanation:
Upgrading to the Enterprise Plus Edition of Cloud SQL fundamentally changes where point-in-time recovery logs are stored. In the standard Enterprise Edition, write-ahead logs are kept on the same persistent disk as the database, which caps the retention window at 7 days and causes storage pressure as logs accumulate. Enterprise Plus automatically offloads these logs to Cloud Storage, which removes the competition between recovery data and active database operations. This architectural shift is what enables support for up to 35 days of point-in-time recovery, directly satisfying the compliance mandate without degrading the performance of the primary database disk.
Increasing the persistent disk size would provide more room temporarily, but it does not change the 7-day retention limit built into the Enterprise tier, nor does it move logs off the primary disk to Cloud Storage. Enabling commit-and-stream settings may affect transaction handling behavior, but this does not alter where recovery logs are physically stored and would not extend the retention window beyond the Enterprise tier maximum. A scheduled pg_dump pipeline produces logical backups rather than continuous point-in-time recovery snapshots, meaning it cannot restore to an arbitrary moment in time and would not integrate with the native Cloud SQL recovery console. Only the edition upgrade addresses both the storage location and the extended retention window simultaneously.

options.text: Enable the commit-and-stream capability on the existing instance
options.explanation:
Upgrading to the Enterprise Plus Edition of Cloud SQL fundamentally changes where point-in-time recovery logs are stored. In the standard Enterprise Edition, write-ahead logs are kept on the same persistent disk as the database, which caps the retention window at 7 days and causes storage pressure as logs accumulate. Enterprise Plus automatically offloads these logs to Cloud Storage, which removes the competition between recovery data and active database operations. This architectural shift is what enables support for up to 35 days of point-in-time recovery, directly satisfying the compliance mandate without degrading the performance of the primary database disk.
Increasing the persistent disk size would provide more room temporarily, but it does not change the 7-day retention limit built into the Enterprise tier, nor does it move logs off the primary disk to Cloud Storage. Enabling commit-and-stream settings may affect transaction handling behavior, but this does not alter where recovery logs are physically stored and would not extend the retention window beyond the Enterprise tier maximum. A scheduled pg_dump pipeline produces logical backups rather than continuous point-in-time recovery snapshots, meaning it cannot restore to an arbitrary moment in time and would not integrate with the native Cloud SQL recovery console. Only the edition upgrade addresses both the storage location and the extended retention window simultaneously.

questions.text: A SaaS platform provider is migrating from multiple on-premises MySQL databases into a single Cloud SQL for MySQL instance. The consolidated database will contain roughly 120,000 tables due to their per-tenant schema isolation model. Performance monitoring of the existing databases shows average resource consumption of about 12 vCPUs and 55 GB of RAM across all tenants combined. Which Cloud SQL instance configuration should you choose to maintain operational reliability and align with Google best practices?

options.text: A Cloud SQL instance with 96 vCPUs and 624 GB of RAM
options.explanation:
The right choice here is the instance with 32 vCPUs and 256 GB of RAM because Cloud SQL for MySQL imposes specific resource requirements that are driven by table count rather than just active query load. When a MySQL instance contains more than 50,000 tables the internal metadata management and data dictionary caching create substantial memory and CPU overhead. Google recommends a minimum of 32 vCPUs and at least 200 GB of memory when operating above 100,000 tables. The 32 vCPU and 256 GB configuration meets these thresholds while being the most economical option that satisfies the constraint. This ensures the InnoDB buffer pool and table definition cache have enough headroom to operate without causing instability or crashes.
The 16 vCPU and 64 GB option appears attractive based on the observed CPU and memory utilization alone but it does not account for the metadata overhead created by 120,000 tables. Running that many tables on an undersized instance would result in painfully slow restarts and out-of-memory failures during schema operations like ALTER TABLE or information_schema queries. The 64 vCPU and 480 GB option would certainly be stable but it exceeds what the table count requires and would generate unnecessary infrastructure costs month over month. Similarly the 96 vCPU and 624 GB option is far more capacity than this workload demands and represents significant over-provisioning with no practical benefit at the 120,000 table level.

questions.text: A retail company recently launched an e-commerce platform on Google Cloud, connecting it to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database. After a successful holiday marketing campaign drove a sharp increase in online orders, the engineering team noticed that checkout queries are experiencing high latency and occasional timeouts, especially during evening hours when traffic surges. Before committing budget to infrastructure changes, you need to understand whether the current Cloud SQL instance can handle the new traffic patterns. What should you do first?

options.text: Analyze the System Insights dashboard to evaluate the instance resource metrics
options.explanation:
The best first step is to review the System Insights dashboard, which provides detailed telemetry on how the Cloud SQL instance is utilizing its allocated CPU, memory, disk I/O, and active connections. This allows the engineering team to identify whether the checkout latency is caused by resource saturation, such as CPU spiking to capacity or memory pressure triggering swap, rather than guessing at the root cause. Making data-driven decisions with real metrics is a fundamental part of any troubleshooting workflow and should always precede costly infrastructure modifications.
Configuring a failover replica is a strategy aimed at availability and disaster recovery, and it would not serve as a diagnostic tool to understand why queries are slow. Vertically scaling the instance may ultimately be the correct resolution, but doing so without first confirming that resource exhaustion is the actual bottleneck could waste money and leave the real issue unresolved, such as poorly written queries or connection pool exhaustion. Implementing the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy is a sound practice that improves connection security and management, but it does not give you visibility into instance-level resource consumption or help you pinpoint the source of the latency problem.
